(24 October 1879 – 28 April 1945) was an Italian [Marxist](/wiki/Marxist "Marxist") revolutionary and later a [fascist](/wiki/Italian_fascism "Italian fascism") politician. He began in the [Italian Socialist Party](/wiki/Italian_Socialist_Party "Italian Socialist Party") as an opponent of the reformist wing and became a founding member of the [Communist Party of Italy](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Italy "Communist Party of Italy") in 1921, sitting on the fifteen-man Central Committee. In his career within the left-wing, he was often called *Il Lenin di Romagna* ([Romagna](/wiki/Romagna "Romagna")'s [Lenin](/wiki/Lenin "Lenin")).[[1]](#cite_note-1) During the latter part of his life, particularly during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), Bombacci allied with [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") and the [Italian Social Republic](/wiki/Italian_Social_Republic "Italian Social Republic") against the [Allied invasion of Italy](/wiki/Allied_invasion_of_Italy "Allied invasion of Italy"). In April 1945 he was shot by communist partisans and his body was subsequently strung up in [Piazzale Loreto](/wiki/Piazzale_Loreto "Piazzale Loreto").

**Nicola Bombacci** (24 October 1879 – 28 April 1945) was an Italian [Marxist](/wiki/Marxist "Marxist") revolutionary and later a [fascist](/wiki/Italian_fascism "Italian fascism") politician. He began in the [Italian Socialist Party](/wiki/Italian_Socialist_Party "Italian Socialist Party") as an opponent of the reformist wing and became a founding member of the [Communist Party of Italy](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Italy "Communist Party of Italy") in 1921, sitting on the fifteen-man Central Committee. In his career within the left-wing, he was often called *Il Lenin di Romagna* ([Romagna](/wiki/Romagna "Romagna")'s [Lenin](/wiki/Lenin "Lenin")).[[1]](#cite_note-1) During the latter part of his life, particularly during the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), Bombacci allied with [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") and the [Italian Social Republic](/wiki/Italian_Social_Republic "Italian Social Republic") against the [Allied invasion of Italy](/wiki/Allied_invasion_of_Italy "Allied invasion of Italy"). In April 1945 he was shot by communist partisans and his body was subsequently strung up in [Piazzale Loreto](/wiki/Piazzale_Loreto "Piazzale Loreto").

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicola_Bombacci&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

### Italian Socialist Party

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicola_Bombacci&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Italian Socialist Party")]

Nicola Bombacci was born near [Forlì](/wiki/Forl%C3%AC "Forlì") on 24 October 1879. During his adolescence he joined the [Italian Socialist Party](/wiki/Italian_Socialist_Party "Italian Socialist Party") (PSI), of which he suddenly became a national leader at the head of the revolutionary wing, known as *Massimalisti*; other notable members of this [far-left](/wiki/Far-left "Far-left") wing were [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini"), who also hailed from the countryside surrounding Forli, and [Costantino Lazzari](/wiki/Costantino_Lazzari "Costantino Lazzari"). In 1919 Bombacci became the Secretary of the PSI and led the socialists in the [general election](/wiki/1919_Italian_general_election "1919 Italian general election") of the same year to their best result ever; the PSI won the election with 32.3% of the vote and became largest party in parliament. The following year, Bombacci resigned as Secretary of the PSI due to some tensions with the right wing of the party led by [Filippo Turati](/wiki/Filippo_Turati "Filippo Turati").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/2nd_World_Congress_of_the_Comintern_Lenin_Zinoviev_Bukharin_Gorky.jpg/250px-2nd_World_Congress_of_the_Comintern_Lenin_Zinoviev_Bukharin_Gorky.jpg)](/wiki/File:2nd_World_Congress_of_the_Comintern_Lenin_Zinoviev_Bukharin_Gorky.jpg)

Bombacci (bearded, seventh from right) with other delegates of the [2nd World Congress of the Comintern](/wiki/2nd_World_Congress_of_the_Comintern "2nd World Congress of the Comintern"), 1920

In 1921 Bombacci became one of the founding fathers of the [Communist Party of Italy](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Italy "Communist Party of Italy") (PCdI).[[2]](#cite_note-smith1994-2) Despite this, due to his days in the PSI as a fellow **Massimalista**, he remained a friend of Mussolini even after the latter had renounced socialism in favour of [fascism](/wiki/Fascism "Fascism"). Bombacci was eventually expelled from the PCdI in 1927 for embracing a pro-fascist position.[[3]](#cite_note-3) From that year onwards, he became an open fascist, although he never officially joined the [National Fascist Party](/wiki/National_Fascist_Party "National Fascist Party"). In the journal *[La Verità](/wiki/La_Verit%C3%A0_(magazine) "La Verità (magazine)")* in 1936, Bombacci confessed "his adhesion to Fascism but also to Communism", writing: "Fascism made a great Social Revolution. Mussolini and Lenin. Corporate and Soviet fascist state, Rome and Moscow. We have gone far enough to regret it, we have nothing to apologize for, for both in the present and in the past we are driven by the same ideal: the triumph of work."[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) By the beginning of the 1940s, Bombacci had officially distanced himself from communism by publishing pamphlets warning the Italian population on the dangers of [Bolshevism](/wiki/Bolshevism "Bolshevism") and how [Stalinism](/wiki/Stalinism "Stalinism") had degenerated socialist values.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Just before the [March on Rome](/wiki/March_on_Rome "March on Rome"), Bombacci became the editor-in-chief of the sole legal edition (dated September 10, 1922) of *Il Fanciullo Proletario* (Proletarian Kid), a Communist illustrated periodical aimed at children.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### Italian Social Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicola_Bombacci&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Italian Social Republic")]

After Mussolini was [ousted](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Fascist_regime_in_Italy "Fall of the Fascist regime in Italy") as Prime Minister of the [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") in 1943, Bombacci remained loyal to Mussolini and tried to help him legitimize the [Italian Social Republic](/wiki/Italian_Social_Republic "Italian Social Republic") and relegitimize [Italian fascism](/wiki/Italian_fascism "Italian fascism").[[8]](#cite_note-8) He joined Mussolini's entourage and frequently visited the dictator at Villa Feltrinelli. According to [R. J. B. Bosworth](/wiki/R._J._B._Bosworth "R. J. B. Bosworth"), Bombacci "almost became a friend" for Mussolini at this time.[[9]](#cite_note-9) He was the author of the economic theory of fascist socialization, prepared for the [1943 Congress of Verona](/wiki/1943_Congress_of_Verona "1943 Congress of Verona"). Nicknamed "the Red Pope" by the bourgeoisie, Bombacci told a crowd in Genoa in 1945 that "Stalin will never make socialism; rather Mussolini will."[[10]](#cite_note-10)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] The project of socialization overseen by Bombacci was viewed with suspicion and boycotted by the occupying Nazi German forces; it was also unpopular among the Italian proletariat, who responded with huge strikes.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Nicola_Bombacci&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Death")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/The_dead_body_of_Benito_Mussolini_Piazzale_Loreto.jpg/250px-The_dead_body_of_Benito_Mussolini_Piazzale_Loreto.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_dead_body_of_Benito_Mussolini_Piazzale_Loreto.jpg)

From left to right, the dead bodies of Bombacci, Gelomini, [Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini"), [Petacci](/wiki/Clara_Petacci "Clara Petacci"), [Pavolini](/wiki/Alessandro_Pavolini "Alessandro Pavolini") and [Starace](/wiki/Achille_Starace "Achille Starace") in [Piazzale Loreto](/wiki/Piazzale_Loreto "Piazzale Loreto"), 1945. At the bottom of the photo it says "justice is done".

Bombacci was shot on 28 April 1945 at [Dongo](/wiki/Dongo,_Lombardy "Dongo, Lombardy") (province of Como) where he had been captured, along with Mussolini, by Italian communist partisans. He was summarily shot alongside Mussolini. Before his execution, Bombacci shouted out "Long live Mussolini! Long live socialism!"[[12]](#cite_note-12) After his death, his corpse was hung upside down at Piazzale Loreto in a public display, along with Mussolini, [Clara Petacci](/wiki/Clara_Petacci "Clara Petacci") (Mussolini's mistress), [Alessandro Pavolini](/wiki/Alessandro_Pavolini "Alessandro Pavolini") (the secretary of the [Republican Fascist Party](/wiki/Republican_Fascist_Party "Republican Fascist Party")), [Achille Starace](/wiki/Achille_Starace "Achille Starace") and others.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Above the body of Bombacci, there was a handwritten sign that read "*Supertraditore*" (Super-Traitor).[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Four days after his death, his old friend and comrade [Victor Serge](/wiki/Victor_Serge "Victor Serge") read of the event in a newspaper in Mexico, where he was living in exile, and wrote several pages in his journal concerning him. Engaging in what he called "practical psychology", Serge tried to imagine how a former communist could become a fascist: "Among some Italians, particularly among the ex-Marxists and ex-syndicalists, two visions became apparent: that with the liberal democracies exhausted and socialism weakened, the corporatist regimes were going to impose their new formulas; and that through this narrow gate would pass collectivism, the precondition for a socialism different from that desired by the nineteenth century, ... corresponding better to man's basic nature."[[16]](#cite_note-16)
